request.question: Which president was present for the Mexican Civil War who originated from Oaxaca?
request.answer: liberal politician of Zapotec origin from Oaxaca.

title: 128th Infantry Regiment (United States)
José María Lafragua
The Epic of American Civilization
History of Oaxaca
The Old Gringo
Aureliano Blanquet
Mexican Youth Athenaeum
Benito Juárez
Agnes Salm-Salm
George Puflea
sentences: The 128th Infantry Regiment ("Les Terribles") is a United States military unit of the Wisconsin National Guard.
The 128th has served as part of the American Civil War, Spanish–American War, Mexican Civil War, World War I, World War II and the Iraq War.
José María Ibarra Lafragua was a Mexican liberal lawyer, politician, diplomat, and writer.
He was born in Puebla, 2 April 1813, the son of Lt. Col. José María Lafragua and Mariana de Ibarra and Veytia.
He served in as Minister of Interior in the government of liberal President Ignacio Comonfort.
He was the first director of the National Library of Mexico and key author of the Mexican Civil and Penal Codes.
The Epic of American Civilization is a mural by the social realist painter José Clemente Orozco.
It is located in the basement reading room of the Baker Memorial Library on the campus of Dartmouth College in Hanover, New Hampshire.

The mural, painted between 1932 and 1934, consists of a series of 24 fresco panels, whose principal themes are the impact of both indigenous Native Americans and European colonists on North America, and the impact of war (particularly the Mexican Civil War and the First World War) and rapid industrialization on the human spirit.
The mural was designated a National Historic Landmark in 2013.
In the Central Valley region of the Southern Mexican state of Oaxaca archeologists discovered evidence of historic settlements.

Aztecs from Tenochtitlan on the volcanic plateau to the North around what today is Mexico City first arrived in this region around 1250 AD establishing military rule in the 15th century until the arrival of the Spanish.
After the fall of Tenochtitlan, the Spanish took over Oaxaca which led to the eventual decrease of the Native population and the increase in African slaves.
The region was then settled by mostly Spanish immigrants from Europe and the African slaves they brought with them.
Oaxaca was considered a department after the Mexican War of Independence, but after the fall of emperor Agustín de Iturbide, it became a state in 1824 with José Murguia as its first governor.
During the 19th century, Oaxaca was split between liberal and conservative factions.
The political and military struggles between the factions resulted in wars and intrigues.
A series of major disasters occurred in the state from the 1920s to the 1940s.
In the 1940s and 1950s, new infrastructure projects were begun.
From the 1980s to the present, there has been much development of the tourism industry in the state.
The Old Gringo (Spanish: "Gringo Viejo" ) is a novel by Carlos Fuentes, written from 1964 to 1984 and first published in 1985.
Fuentes stated: "What started this novel was my admiration for Ambrose Bierce and for his "Tales of Soldiers and Civilians".
I was fascinated with the idea of a man who fought in the United States Civil War and dies in a Mexican civil war."
The novel addresses themes of death, cultural exchange, and Mexican identity, among others.
Its English-language translation became the first novel by a Mexican author to become a U.S. bestseller.
The book was one of three nominees for the Ritz Paris Hemingway Award as best novel of 1985.
Aureliano Blanquet (Morelia, Michoacán, 31 December 1849 – 15 April 1919 Barranca de Chavaxtla, Huatusco, Veracruz) was a general of the Federal Army during the Mexican Civil War.
He was a key participant in the coup d'état during the Decena trágica.
Blanquet has been identified "as one of the major villains of the Mexican Revolution".

The Mexican Youth Athenaeum, later known as the "Athenaeum of Mexico", was a Mexican civil association founded on October 28, 1909 with the purpose of working in favor of culture and art, by means of organization public meetings and debates.

Born as a response of a generation of young intellectuals who in the decline of the rule of President Porfirio Díaz set a series of criticisms to determinism and mechanism set by the Comtian and Spencerian positivism as the development model of Porfirio Díaz's administration and the group of the "científicos. Through a series of conferences and different cultural efforts they activated a reflexive awareness on education.
Benito Pablo Juárez García (] ; 21 March 1806 – 18 July 1872) was a Mexican lawyer and liberal politician of Zapotec origin from Oaxaca.

Agnes Salm-Salm (December 25, 1844 – December 21, 1912) was the American wife of Prince Felix zu Salm-Salm, a Prussian mercenary beside whom she played a role in the American Civil War, the Mexican Civil War between President Benito Juárez and the Austrian archduke Maximilian I of Mexico, and the Franco-Prussian War.
George Puflea Craciun was an early aviator.
Born in modern-day Romania, he flew in the First Balkan War, in the Mexican Revolution, in the First World War and in early American and Mexican civil aviation.
He was also known under his name in Spanish, .

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Which president was present for the Mexican Civil War who originated from Oaxaca?
hop1_docs: History of Oaxaca | In the Central Valley region of the Southern Mexican state of Oaxaca archeologists discovered evidence of historic settlements.  Aztecs from Tenochtitlan on the volcanic plateau to the North around what today is Mexico City first arrived in this region around 1250 AD establishing military rule in the 15th century until the arrival of the Spanish.  After the fall of Tenochtitlan, the Spanish took over Oaxaca which led to the eventual decrease of the Native population and the increase in African slaves.  The region was then settled by mostly Spanish immigrants from Europe and the African slaves they brought with them.  Oaxaca was considered a department after the Mexican War of Independence, but after the fall of emperor Agustín de Iturbide, it became a state in 1824 with José Murguia as its first governor.  During the 19th century, Oaxaca was split between liberal and conservative factions.  The political and military struggles between the factions resulted in wars and intrigues.  A series of major disasters occurred in the state from the 1920s to the 1940s.  In the 1940s and 1950s, new infrastructure projects were begun.  From the 1980s to the present, there has been much development of the tourism industry in the state.

Capture of Oaxaca (1812) | The Capture of Oaxaca was a battle during the War of Mexican Independence that occurred on 25 November 1812 at Oaxaca, Oaxaca.  It was fought between the royalist forces loyal to the Spanish crown, commanded by General Antonio Pío González-Saravia Mollinedo, and the Mexican rebels fighting for independence from the Spanish Empire, commanded by José María Morelos y Pavón.  The battle resulted in a victory for the Mexican rebels.

Félix Díaz (politician) | Félix Díaz Velasco (17 February 18689 July 1945) was a Mexican politician and general born in Oaxaca, Oaxaca.  He graduated as an engineer from the Colegio Militar in 1888.  He was a leading figure in the rebellion against President Francisco I. Madero during the Mexican Revolution.

Marquisate of the Valley of Oaxaca | The Marquisate of the Valley of Oaxaca (Spanish: "Marquesado del Valle de Oaxaca" ) is a hereditary marquisal title in the Spanish nobility and a former seignorial estate in New Spain.  It was granted to Don Hernán Cortés, "conquistador" who led the conquest of the Aztec Empire, by Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor in 1529.  Despite its name, the marquisate covered a much larger area than the Valley of Oaxaca, comprising a vast stretch of land in the present-day Mexican states of Oaxaca, Morelos, Veracruz, Michoacán and Mexico.

Copa de Zaachila | The Copa de Zaachila is presented each year by the President of the Mexican Baseball League to the team that wins the Serie del Rey.  Recent trophy designs contain the design of a china cup found in the archeological zone of Zaachila, Oaxaca.

Libyan Civil War (2011–present) | The Libyan Civil War refers to the ongoing conflicts in Libya, beginning with the Arab Spring protests of 2011, which led to the First Libyan Civil War, foreign military intervention, and the ousting and death of Muammar Gaddafi.  The civil war's aftermath and proliferation of armed groups led to violence and instability across the country, which erupted into renewed civil war in 2014.  The ongoing crisis in Libya has so far resulted in tens of thousands of casualties since the onset of violence in early 2011.  During both civil wars, the output of Libya's economically crucial oil industry collapsed to a small fraction of its usual level, with most facilities blockaded or damaged by rival groups, despite having the largest oil reserves of any African country.  U.S. President Barack Obama stated on 11 April 2016 that not preparing for a post-Gaddafi Libya was probably the "worst mistake" of his presidency.

Monte Albán | Monte Albán, likely originally called Danipaguache (Zapotec for sacred mountain of life), is a large pre-Columbian archaeological site in the Santa Cruz Xoxocotlán Municipality in the southern Mexican state of Oaxaca (17.043° N, 96.767°W).  The site is located on a low mountainous range rising above the plain in the central section of the Valley of Oaxaca where the latter's northern Etla, eastern Tlacolula, and southern Zimatlán & Ocotlán (or Valle Grande) branches meet.  The present-day state capital Oaxaca City is located approximately 9 km east of Monte Albán.
hop1_titles: Marquisate of the Valley of Oaxaca
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- "José Murguia as its first governor." — History of Oaxaca [1]
- "emperor Agustín de Iturbide" — History of Oaxaca [1]
- "25 November 1812" — Capture of Oaxaca (1812) [2]
- "José María Morelos y Pavón" — Capture of Oaxaca (1812) [2]
- "Félix Díaz Velasco (17 February 18689 July 1945)" — Félix Díaz (politician) [3]
- "born in Oaxaca, Oaxaca." — Félix Díaz (politician) [3]
- "President Francisco I. Madero" — Félix Díaz (politician) [3]
- "the Mexican Revolution." — Félix Díaz (politician) [3]
- "Oaxaca (17.043° N, 96.767°W)" — Monte Albán [7]
- "Don Hernán Cortés, \"conquistador\"" — Marquisate of the Valley of Oaxaca [4]

Ambiguities:
- The term "Mexican Civil War" is not defined in the passages and could refer to multiple conflicts: "War of Mexican Independence" (Capture of Oaxaca [2]), "the Mexican Revolution." (Félix Díaz [3]), or 19th-century "liberal and conservative" struggles (History of Oaxaca [1]).
- Named persons in passages include "Félix Díaz" (dates token malformed "18689 July 1945") and presidents referenced ("President Francisco I. Madero", "emperor Agustín de Iturbide") but none are explicitly identified as a president born in Oaxaca in these passages.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing the identity of a Mexican president who was born in Oaxaca and confirmation that this president was "present for the Mexican Civil War" (i.e., which civil conflict is meant and whether that president participated or was in office then). The passages do not state a president's birthplace = Oaxaca and their presence/involvement in a specific civil war.

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch page "Porfirio Díaz" (check birthplace and role at the start of the Mexican Revolution).
- Fetch page "Benito Juárez" (check birthplace and role during the Reform War/19th-century civil conflicts).
- Fetch page "Mexican Civil War (disambiguation)" or run query "Mexican Civil War meaning 'War of the Reform' or 'Mexican Revolution'".
- Run query "President of Mexico born in Oaxaca" or fetch "List of presidents of Mexico" and check birthplaces for Oaxaca.
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
response.hop2_query: president born Oaxaca Mexican Civil War
hop2_docs: Félix Díaz (politician) | Félix Díaz Velasco (17 February 18689 July 1945) was a Mexican politician and general born in Oaxaca, Oaxaca.  He graduated as an engineer from the Colegio Militar in 1888.  He was a leading figure in the rebellion against President Francisco I. Madero during the Mexican Revolution.

Capture of Oaxaca (1812) | The Capture of Oaxaca was a battle during the War of Mexican Independence that occurred on 25 November 1812 at Oaxaca, Oaxaca.  It was fought between the royalist forces loyal to the Spanish crown, commanded by General Antonio Pío González-Saravia Mollinedo, and the Mexican rebels fighting for independence from the Spanish Empire, commanded by José María Morelos y Pavón.  The battle resulted in a victory for the Mexican rebels.

Zapotec civilization | The Zapotec civilization was an indigenous pre-Columbian civilization that flourished in the Valley of Oaxaca in Mesoamerica.  Archaeological evidence shows that their culture goes back at least 2,500 years.  The Zapotec left archaeological evidence at the ancient city of Monte Albán in the form of buildings, ball courts, magnificent tombs and grave goods including finely worked gold jewelry.  Monte Albán was one of the first major cities in Mesoamerica and the center of a Zapotec state that dominated much of the territory that today belongs to the Mexican state of Oaxaca.

Fernando Larrazábal Bretón | Fernando Larrazábal Bretón (born August 11, 1962) is a Mexican politician born in Oaxaca who has served as mayor of San Nicolás and was a member of Nuevo León's Congress in 2009.  He was municipal president of Monterrey between 2009 and 2012.
Jesús Henestrosa | Jesús Henestrosa Vega (born June 19, 1994 in Oaxaca City, Oaxaca) is a Mexican professional footballer who plays for Chapulineros de Oaxaca.

Agnes Salm-Salm | Agnes Salm-Salm (December 25, 1844 – December 21, 1912) was the American wife of Prince Felix zu Salm-Salm, a Prussian mercenary beside whom she played a role in the American Civil War, the Mexican Civil War between President Benito Juárez and the Austrian archduke Maximilian I of Mexico, and the Franco-Prussian War.

Adolfo Toledo Infanzón | Adolfo Toledo Infanzón (born in Ciudad Ixtepec, Oaxaca on March 24, 1961), is a Mexican politician, member of the Institutional Revolutionary Party and the current Senator in the LXI Legislature of the Mexican Congress from the State of Oaxaca.
